title: How Yasujiré Ozu Learned to Use Color in His Masterful Films: A New Every Frame a Painting Video Essay
description: YasuôÙjiré Ozu was born in 1903, and made films from the late nineôÙteen-twenôÙties up until his death in 1963. Though not an espeôÙcialôÙly long
body:
YasuôÙjiré Ozu was born in 1903, and made films from the late nineôÙteen-twenôÙties up until his death in 1963. Though not an espeôÙcialôÙly long life, it spanned Japanãs pre- and postôÙwar eras, meanôÙing that in many ways, it endôÙed in a very difôÙferôÙent counôÙtry than it began. Not that youãd know it from Ozuãs films, whose disôÙtincôÙtive form and style must have changed less through the decades than those of any of his colôÙleagues. For viewôÙers only casuôÙalôÙly acquaintôÙed with his oeuôÙvre, itãs easy to joke that if youãve seen one of his picôÙtures, youãve seen them all. But true Ozu enthuôÙsiôÙasts, whose numôÙbers have steadiôÙly grown all around the world since the filmôÙmakôÙerãs death, underôÙstand that each phase of his career offers disôÙtincôÙtive pleaôÙsures of its own.
In fact, Ozu perôÙsistôÙed through sweepôÙing changes in not just world hisôÙtoôÙry, but also the hisôÙtoôÙry of cinôÙeôÙma. His first 34 films were silent, the next fourôÙteen were sound in black-and-white, and his last six were in colôÙor. It is to the domesôÙtic masôÙterãs third act that Tony Zhou and TayôÙlor Ramos have devotôÙed their latôÙestô Every Frame a PaintôÙing video essay.
As with most filmôÙmakôÙers, it took Ozu a few years to make colôÙor his own: in Equinox Flower, from 1958, ãsome of the scenes are so bright that it looks like an MGM musiôÙcal,ã owing to his stuôÙdioãs desire to showôÙcase the actress Fujiko YamamoôÙto. And itãs not just the hues of her kimono that domôÙiôÙnate the images: so does the red of Ozuãs sigôÙnaôÙture teapot whenôÙevôÙer it finds its way into the frame.
Ozuãs next colôÙor filmô Good MornôÙing makes use of a ãmuch more natôÙurôÙal, earth-toned colôÙor palette. The images feel more balôÙanced, and there isnãt one visuôÙal eleôÙment that sticks out from all the othôÙers.ã In his project after that, FloatôÙing Weeds (itself a remake of his 1934 silent A StoôÙry of FloatôÙing Weeds), he worked with the acclaimed cinôÙeôÙmatogôÙraôÙphôÙer Kazuo MiyaôÙgawa, whoãd also colôÙlabôÙoôÙratôÙed with the likes of KuroôÙsawa and Mizoguchi. Using strong light and shadôÙow, MiyaôÙgawa showed how, ãby shapôÙing the light, he could change how colôÙors were perôÙceived,ã often in difôÙferôÙent scenes framed in exactôÙly the same way. At this point, anyôÙone doing an Ozu binge-watch will feel that colôÙor itself is being adaptôÙed to the rigôÙorôÙous objecôÙtivôÙiôÙty of his work.


	

ãHis films are full of repôÙeôÙtiôÙtions and small variôÙaôÙtions,ã Zhou says. ãHe will show the same hallôÙway again, and again, and again.ã SeemôÙingôÙly minor eleôÙments in one scene match visuôÙalôÙly with eleôÙments in othôÙers. ãAs a result, Ozuãs movies rhyme. One shot will mirôÙror anothôÙer, one perôÙsonôÙãs behavôÙior will be repeatôÙed,ã across not just an indiôÙvidôÙual picôÙture, but his whole filôÙmogôÙraôÙphy. Watch through it, andô ãyouãre struck by how simôÙiôÙlar two peoôÙple can be, how often one place resemôÙbles anothôÙer, how life itself is cycliôÙcal, and Ozu used colôÙor as anothôÙer way to build these patôÙterns.ã Though subôÙtly expressed, these themes would cerôÙtainôÙly have resôÙonatôÙed with audiôÙences in a sociôÙety forced to reinôÙvent itself after losôÙing the SecôÙond World War. Whether Ozu susôÙpectôÙed that they could draw even more attenôÙtion from future genôÙerôÙaôÙtions far from Japan is a quesôÙtionô not even his diaries, now the subôÙject of a docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary themôÙselves, can answer.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
An IntroôÙducôÙtion to YasuôÙjiré Ozu, ãthe Most JapanôÙese of All Film DirecôÙtorsã
How One SimôÙple Cut Reveals the CinôÙeôÙmatôÙic Genius of YasuôÙjiré Ozu
The GoldôÙen Age of JapanôÙese CinôÙeôÙma: KuroôÙsawa, Ozu, Mizoguchi & Beyond
Wes AnderôÙson & YasuôÙjiro Ozu: New Video Essay Reveals the UnexôÙpectôÙed ParôÙalôÙlels Between Two Great FilmôÙmakôÙers
How MasôÙter JapanôÙese AniôÙmaôÙtor Satoshi Kon Pushed the BoundôÙaries of MakôÙing AniôÙme: A Video Essay
Every Frame a PaintôÙing Returns to YouTube & Explores Why the SusôÙtained Two-Shot VanôÙished from Movies
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Buckminster Fuller Creates an Animated Visualization of Human Population Growth from 1000 B.C.E. to 1965
description:
in Design, Maps |   	May 7th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Sit back, relax, put on some music (Iãve found Chopinãs NocôÙturne in B major well-suitôÙed),
body:
in Design, Maps |   	May 7th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Sit back, relax, put on some music (Iãve found Chopinãs NocôÙturne in B major well-suitôÙed), and watch the video above, a silent data visuôÙalôÙizaôÙtion by visionôÙary archiôÙtect and sysôÙtems theôÙoôÙrist BuckôÙminôÙster Fuller, ãthe James Brown of indusôÙtriôÙal design.ã The short film from 1965 comôÙbines two of Fullerãs leadôÙing conôÙcerns: the expoôÙnenôÙtial spreadô of the human popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion over finite massôÙes of land and the need to revise our globôÙal perôÙspecôÙtive via theô ãDymaxôÙion map,ã in order ãto visuôÙalôÙize the whole planôÙet with greater accuôÙraôÙcy,ã as the BuckôÙminôÙster Fuller InstiôÙtute writes, so that ãwe humans will be betôÙter equipped to address chalôÙlenges as we face our comôÙmon future aboard SpaceôÙship Earth.ã
Though you may know it best as the name of a geoôÙdesôÙic sphere at Disneyãs Epcot CenôÙter, the term SpaceôÙship Earth origôÙiôÙnalôÙly came from Fuller, who used it to remind us of our interôÙconôÙnectôÙedôÙness and interôÙdeôÙpenôÙdence as we share resources on the only vehiôÙcle we know of that can susôÙtain us in the cosôÙmos.
ãWe are all astroôÙnauts,ã he wrote in his 1969 OperôÙatôÙing ManôÙuôÙal for SpaceôÙship Earth, and yet we refuse to see the long-term conôÙseôÙquences of our actions on our speôÙcialôÙized craft: ãOne of the reaôÙsons why we are strugôÙgling inadôÙeôÙquateôÙly today,ã Fuller argued in his introôÙducôÙtion, ãis that we reckôÙon our costs on too shortôÙsightôÙed a basis and are latôÙer overôÙwhelmed with the unexôÙpectôÙed costs brought about by our shortôÙsightôÙedôÙness.ã

Like all visionôÙarôÙies, Fuller thought in long spans of time, and he used his design skills to help othôÙersô do so as well. His popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion visuôÙalôÙizaôÙtionô docôÙuôÙments humanô growth from 1000 B.C.E. to Fullerãs present, at the time, of 1965. In the image above (see a largôÙer verôÙsion here), we have a graphôÙic from that same yearãmade colôÙlabôÙoôÙraôÙtiveôÙly with artist and sociôÙolôÙoôÙgist John McHaleãshowing the ãshrinkôÙing of our planôÙet by manãs increased travôÙel and comôÙmuôÙniôÙcaôÙtion speeds around the globe.ã (It must be near microôÙscopôÙic by now.) Fuller takes an even longer view, lookôÙing at ãthe conôÙfluôÙence of comôÙmuôÙniôÙcaôÙtion and transôÙportaôÙtion techôÙnoloôÙgies,ã writes Rikke Schmidt KjûÎrôÙgaard, ãfrom 500,000 B.C.E. to 1965.ã
Here Fuller comôÙbines his popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion data with the techôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal breakôÙthroughs of moderôÙniôÙty. Though heãs thought of in some quarôÙters as a genius and in some as a kook, Fuller demonôÙstratôÙed his tremenôÙdous foreôÙsight in seemôÙingôÙly innuôÙmerôÙable ways. But it was in the realm of design that he excelled in comôÙmuôÙniôÙcatôÙing what he saw. ãPioôÙneers of data visuôÙalôÙizaôÙtion,ã Fuller and McHale were two of ãthe first to chart long-term trends of indusôÙtriôÙalôÙizaôÙtion and globôÙalôÙizaôÙtion.ã Instead of becomôÙing alarmed and fearôÙful of what the trends showed, Fuller got to work designôÙing for the future, fulôÙly aware, writes the Fuller InstiôÙtute, that ãthe planôÙet is a sysôÙtem, and a resilient one.ã
Fuller thought like a radôÙiôÙcalôÙly invenôÙtive engiôÙneer, but he spoke and wrote like a peacenik prophet, writôÙing that a sysôÙtem of narôÙrow speôÙcialôÙizaôÙtions ensures that skill sets ãare not comôÙpreôÙhendôÙed comôÙpreôÙhenôÙsiveôÙlyãÎ or they are realôÙized only in negôÙaôÙtive ways, in new weaponôÙry or the indusôÙtriôÙal supôÙport only of war farôÙing.ã Weãve seen this vision of sociôÙety played out to a frightôÙenôÙing extent. Fuller saw a way out, one in which everyôÙone on the planôÙet can live in comôÙfort and secuôÙriôÙty withôÙout conôÙsumôÙing (then not renewôÙing) the Earthãs resources. How can this be done? Youãll have to read Fullerãs work to find out. MeanôÙwhile, as his visuôÙalôÙizaôÙtions sugôÙgest, itãs best for us to take the long viewãand give up on short-term rewards and profitsãin our assessôÙments of the state of SpaceôÙship Earth.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
BuckôÙminôÙster Fullerãs Map of the World: The InnoôÙvaôÙtion that RevôÙoôÙluôÙtionôÙized Map Design (1943)
How the Human PopôÙuôÙlaôÙtion Reached 8 BilôÙlion: An AniôÙmatôÙed Video CovôÙers 300,000 Years of HisôÙtoôÙry in Four MinôÙutes
The Life & Times of BuckôÙminôÙster Fullerãs GeoôÙdesôÙic Dome: A DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtary
A VisuôÙalôÙizaôÙtion of the UnitôÙed Statesã ExplodôÙing PopôÙuôÙlaôÙtion Growth Over 200 Years (1790 ã 2010)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.

title: 1,000 Years of Medieval European History in 20 Minutes
description:
in History |   	May 6th, 2026  3 Comments
	
			

	

More than a few medievalôÙists object to the term ãDark Agesã as applied to the periôÙod in which
body:
in History |   	May 6th, 2026  3 Comments
	
			

	

More than a few medievalôÙists object to the term ãDark Agesã as applied to the periôÙod in which they speôÙcialôÙize. That can seem wishôÙful in light of most comôÙparôÙisons between medieval times and the RenaisôÙsance that came afterôÙward, or indeed, the era of the Roman Empire that came before. ConôÙsidôÙer the state of Europe as the fourth cenôÙtuôÙry began: ãThe great cities of antiqôÙuiôÙty were depopôÙuôÙlatôÙed, some left in ruins,ã says the narôÙraôÙtor of the How So video above, telling the stoôÙry of the conôÙtiôÙnenôÙtãs politôÙiôÙcal and linôÙguisôÙtic fragôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion. ãThe Roman transôÙportaôÙtion sysôÙtem decayed, erodôÙing comôÙmuôÙniôÙcaôÙtion and long-disôÙtance trade. Coins vanôÙished, leavôÙing no ecoôÙnomôÙic sysôÙtem to supôÙport proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal armies. LitôÙerôÙaôÙcy plumôÙmetôÙed, cripôÙpling adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtive sysôÙtems. And most notably, peace and secuôÙriôÙty were gone.ã
But thereãs plenôÙty more hisôÙtoôÙry to come thereôÙafter: about a milôÙlenôÙniôÙumãs worth, in fact, which the video covôÙers in a mere twenôÙty minôÙutes. Events of note in that grand sweep include JusôÙtinôÙian Iãs attempt to expand the ByzanôÙtine Empire of the east; the creôÙation and spread of the IslamôÙic caliphate; CharleôÙmagôÙneãs uniôÙfiôÙcaôÙtion of most of westôÙern ChrisôÙtenôÙdom; invaôÙsions by Vikings, MagôÙyars, and MusôÙlim raiders; the rise of casôÙtles and the feuôÙdal sysôÙtem that they came to symôÙbolôÙize; the creôÙation of the Holy Roman Empire; the flourôÙishôÙing of cities and uniôÙverôÙsiôÙties; and the NorôÙman ConôÙquest of EngôÙland, as seen on the Bayeux TapesôÙtry. Thereãs also the unpleasôÙantôÙness of the Black Death, which swept through Europe from the mid-fourôÙteenth to the earôÙly sixôÙteenth cenôÙtuôÙry ã butô as with othôÙer medieval disôÙasôÙters, the plague held the seeds of a civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtionôÙal rebirth.
ãFor some surôÙvivors, the conôÙseôÙquences of the plague were not so grim,ã says the narôÙraôÙtor. ãAs the popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion dropped, land became wideôÙly availôÙable, and the demand for labor rose draôÙmatôÙiôÙcalôÙly.ã PeasôÙants demandôÙed improved conôÙdiôÙtions and revoltôÙed against the rulers who refused; ultiôÙmateôÙly, they ãgained new freeôÙdoms and opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties, and workôÙers enjoyed highôÙer wages. CreôÙativôÙiôÙty and innoôÙvaôÙtion in sciôÙence and culôÙture folôÙlowed, creôÙatôÙing the enviôÙronôÙment in which EuroôÙpean scholôÙars ãdefined the past milôÙlenôÙniôÙum as ãDark Ages,ã and so posiôÙtioned themôÙselves as the tranôÙsiôÙtion between the medieval and modôÙern world.ã Some liken the curôÙrent state of the world to the decline of the Roman Empire; if theyãre corôÙrect, maybe we have anothôÙer RenaisôÙsance to look forôÙward to about 40 genôÙerôÙaôÙtions down the road.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
A Free Yale Course on Medieval HisôÙtoôÙry: 700 Years in 22 LecôÙtures
What Did PeoôÙple Eat in Medieval Times? A Video Series and New CookôÙbook Explain
How EveryôÙthing in a Medieval CasôÙtle Worked, from Its Moats to Its DunôÙgeons
What Sex Was Like in Medieval Times?: HisôÙtoôÙriôÙans Look at How PeoôÙple Got It On in the Dark Ages
How the ByzanôÙtine Empire Rose, Fell, and CreôÙatôÙed the GloôÙriôÙous Hagia Sophia: A HisôÙtoôÙry in Ten AniôÙmatôÙed MinôÙutes
Advice for Time TravôÙelôÙing to Medieval Europe: How to Stay Healthy & Safe, and AvoidôÙing Charges of WitchôÙcraft
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Confidence: The Cartoon That Helped America Get Through the Great Depression (1933)
description:
in Animation, History, Music |   	May 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

No more bumôÙminã, letãs all get to workãÎ
ActuôÙalôÙly, hold up a sec. Weãll
body:
in Animation, History, Music |   	May 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

No more bumôÙminã, letãs all get to workãÎ
ActuôÙalôÙly, hold up a sec. Weãll all be hapôÙpiôÙer and more proôÙducôÙtive if we take a moment to start our work day with ConôÙfiôÙdence, a pepôÙpy musiôÙcal aniôÙmaôÙtion from 1933, starôÙring newôÙly electôÙed PresôÙiôÙdentô Franklin Delano RooôÙsevelt and MickôÙey Mouse preôÙcurôÙsor,ô Oswald the Lucky RabôÙbit.ô 
Few Americansãtoday weãd refer to them as the 1%ãcould escape the priôÙvaôÙtions of the Great DepresôÙsion.ô The movies were one indusôÙtry that conôÙtinôÙued to thrive through this dark periôÙod, preôÙciseôÙly because they offered a few hours of respite. No one went to the picôÙtures to see a reflecôÙtion of their own lives. GorôÙgeous gowns, glamôÙorous ManôÙhatôÙtan apartôÙments and romanôÙtic trouôÙble cerôÙtainô to be resolved in hapôÙpy endingsãÎrememberô Mia FarôÙrowãs beleaôÙgueredô waitôÙressô baskôÙing inô the PurôÙple Rose of Cairoãsô reasôÙsurôÙing glow?
GivôÙen the pubôÙlicãs prefôÙerôÙence for escapist fare, direcôÙtor Bill Nolan, the Father of RubôÙber Hose AniôÙmaôÙtion, could have played it safe by glossôÙing over the backôÙstoôÙry that leads Oswald to seek out advice fromô the ComôÙmanôÙder in Chief. Instead, Nolan delivôÙered his joyôÙful carôÙtoon aniôÙmals into nightôÙmare terôÙriôÙtoôÙry, the DepresôÙsion perôÙsonôÙiôÙfied as a cowled Death figôÙure layôÙing waste to the land. Itãs weirdôÙly upsetôÙting to see those hyper-cheerôÙful vinôÙtage barnôÙyard aniôÙmals (and a rogue monôÙkey) underôÙgo this graphôÙicô enerôÙvaôÙtion.
Oh, for some oral historyãIãd love to know how matiôÙnee crowds reactôÙed as Oswald raced screamôÙing before a spinôÙning verôÙtiôÙgo backôÙground, seekôÙing a remôÙeôÙdy for a host of non-carôÙtoon probôÙlems. Irony is a luxôÙuôÙry they didôÙnãt have.
UnsurôÙprisôÙingôÙly,ô the can-do spirôÙit so cenôÙtral to FDRãs New Deal quickôÙly turned Oswaldãs frown upside down. As presôÙiôÙdenôÙtial camôÙpaign promisôÙes go, this oneãs uniqueôÙlyô taiôÙlored to the demands ofô musiôÙcal comôÙeôÙdy. WitôÙness Annie, in which the 32nd presôÙiôÙdent wasô again called uponô to Rex HarôÙriôÙson his way into audiôÙence hearts, this time fromô the wheelôÙchair the creôÙators of ConôÙfiôÙdence didôÙnãt dare show, some forty years earôÙliôÙer.
The diviôÙsion between enterôÙtainôÙment and nation-leadôÙing is pretôÙty perôÙmeôÙable these days, too.
AccordôÙingôÙly, what realôÙly sets this carôÙtoon apart for me is the use of a PresôÙiôÙdenôÙtialôÙly-sancôÙtioned giant syringe as a tool to get DepresôÙsion-era AmerôÙiôÙca back on its feet. A figôÙuôÙraôÙtive injecôÙtion of conôÙfiôÙdence is all well and good, but nothôÙing gets the barnôÙyardô back on its singing, dancôÙing feet like a libôÙerôÙal dose, delivôÙered in the most litôÙerôÙal way.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2014.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
A SimôÙple, Down-to-Earth ChristôÙmas Card from the Great DepresôÙsion (1933)
PriôÙvate SnaôÙfu: The World War II ProôÙpaôÙganôÙda CarôÙtoons CreôÙatôÙed by Dr. Seuss, Frank Capra & Mel Blanc
Yale Presents an Archive of 170,000 PhoôÙtographs DocôÙuôÙmentôÙing the Great DepresôÙsion
Great DepresôÙsion CookôÙing: Get BudôÙget-MindôÙed Meals from the Online CookôÙing Show CreôÙatôÙed by 93-Year-Old Clara CanôÙnucôÙciaôÙri
When Al Capone Opened a Soup Kitchen DurôÙing the Great DepresôÙsion: AnothôÙer Side of the LegôÙendary Mobsterãs OperôÙaôÙtion
Ayun HalôÙlôÙiôÙday canãt get enough of that rubôÙber style.ô

title: How Fatinha Ramos Channels ãVisual Activismã in Her Richly Layered Illustrations
description: ãTo me, being a visual activist means I only illustrate stories that resonate with me deeply, by giving voice to minorities or social situations t
body:
ãTo me, being a visual activist means I only illustrate stories that resonate with me deeply, by giving voice to minorities or social situations that need to be addressed,ã says Fatinha Ramos. ãIt is the only way I can truly connect with others.ã
Based in Antwerp, the Portuguese artist and illustrator is well-known for blending analog and digital techniques to create rich, emotive compositions. Collaborating with clients like The New York Times, The Washington Post, Tate, and Scientific American, among many others, Ramos has cultivated a keen eye for storytelling through her distinctive visual language.

Recent partnerships include the Anne Frank Museum and MoMA, the latter of which commissioned the artist to illustrate an essay on her experience being compared to Frida Kahlo. Ramos was born with osteogenesis imperfecta, commonly called brittle bone disease, which spurred a childhood spent in and out of hospitals. Drawing and art-making quickly became a preferred pastime, allowing her to transport herself from such clinical settings.
This adolescent hobby stuck, and Ramos worked as an art director in advertising and publishing for 12 years before venturing out on her own. Boasting an impressive list of clients and collaborators, she considers her practice to be an antidote to stereotypical narratives, whether related to the climate crisis, sexism, racism, or the dire lack of empathy that seems rampant in todayãs world.
ãI want to move away from the narrative that turns artists with disabilities into symbols of resilience,ã she tells MoMA. ãCreativity does not happen despite limitations but through them. Art should broaden how we see the worldãand that includes how we see bodies, too.ã
At the moment, Ramos is only accepting select illustration clients as she focuses on her fine art practice, including a series of anatomical glass sculptures based on brittle bone disease. You can explore more of her practice on her website and Instagram.

schema.headline: How Fatinha Ramos Channels ãVisual Activismã in Her Richly Layered Illustrations

title: Irina Werning Chronicles 18 Years of Photographing 'Las Pelilargas' in a New Book
description: For the better part of two decades, Irina Werning has traveled throughout Latin America searching for a specific trait: incredibly long hair. In h
body:
For the better part of two decades, Irina Werning has traveled throughout Latin America searching for a specific trait: incredibly long hair. In her photography series Las Pelilargasãmeaning ãthe long-haired onesã in Spanishãshe chronicles a time-honored Indigenous tradition through a visual celebration of patience, joy, and cultural pride.
In a statement, Werning shares that when she asks young women in the many small towns sheãs visited why they have long hair, they respond with simple reasons akin to, ãBecause I like it.ã But, Werning adds, ãThe true reason is invisible and passes from generation to generation. Itãs the culture of Latin America, where our ancestors believed that cutting hair was cutting life, that hair is the physical manifestation of our thoughts and our souls and our connection to the land.ã

Nearly 90 images are included in Werningãs new book, Las Pelilargas, published by GOST Books. The photos span 18 years, starting with the artistãs first encounter with long-haired women in 2006 in Argentina, when she was photographing members of the Indigenous Kolla community. 
ãGuided by her intuition, she went on to spend months in remote mountain towns putting up signs in schools, hospitals, and markets, and organising hair competitions in an effort to seek out those with long hair,ã GOST says. Werning continued to make the portraits until 2024.ô ãShe found that traditions were not just surviving, but evolvingô with long hair symbolising bothô continuity and subtle rebellion.ã
Find your copy on Bookshop. You might also enjoy Celia D. Lunaãs series, Cholitas Bravas.

schema.headline: Irina Werning Chronicles 18 Years of Photographing 'Las Pelilargas' in a New Book

title: NASA Just Dropped More Than 12,000 Photos from the Artemis II Mission
description: When it comes to photo dumps, NASA has upped the ante. The organization has added thousands of snapshots from the Artemis II mission to the Gatewa
body:
When it comes to photo dumps, NASA has upped the ante. The organization has added thousands of snapshots from the Artemis II mission to the Gateway to Astronaut Photography of Earth archive. The album now holds 12,217 images by cosmic travelers Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen during their more than 250,000-mile, 10-day flyby mission around the moon.
According to PetaPixel, a couple of Nikons and an iPhone 17 were the cameras of choice for the journey. And even though many of the thousands of recently uploaded images are very similarãsome are even quite blurryãscrolling through them gives the impression of being seated right next to the ãMoonfarersã as they marvel at Earth and its satellite and simply canãt put the camera downãjust like we tend to snap way too many photos of a beautiful sunset.

Some of the most impactful photos include the reflections of the astronautsã hands and faces in the window of their vehicle, the Orion module. Juxtaposed with meticulously engineered equipment, the earth and moon seem somehow less abstracted from this unique vantage point, in which these orbs appear somehow more resonant and preciousãand vulnerable.
Explore more of our favorites below, and learn about the Artemis II mission on NASAãs site.

schema.headline: NASA Just Dropped More Than 12,000 Photos from the Artemis II Mission
